name: Helicone LLM Observability MCP Server
what_it_does: MCP server for Helicone — the open-source LLM observability and analytics platform. Enables AI agents to query LLM request logs, analyze costs, monitor model performance, and retrieve usage analytics. Helicone works as a proxy logging all LLM API calls with full request/response capture, latency tracking, and cost attribution.
use_cases: Querying LLM request logs and usage analytics from AI cost monitoring agents
Analyzing model performance and error rates from AI observability agents
Tracking prompt version performance and A/B test results from AI evaluation agents
Monitoring LLM costs per user/project from AI billing management agents
not_for: Production LLM routing (Helicone is observability, not a full gateway)
best_when: An AI developer team wants observability into LLM usage — tracking costs, latency, error rates, and prompt performance across OpenAI, Anthropic, and other providers.
avoid_when: You need a full LLM gateway with routing and failover — use Portkey or LiteLLM instead. PRIVACY: LLM prompts and responses pass through Helicone.

auth.notes: Helicone API key authentication. Separate read and write keys available. Keys managed in Helicone dashboard.

pricing.notes: Generous free tier for individual developers. Open source for self-hosting. Cloud hosted for convenience.

performance.rate_limits: API query rate limits depend on plan

known_agent_gotchas: PRIVACY: LLM prompts and responses are logged — review data retention and sensitivity
Self-hosted Helicone available for data sovereignty requirements
Community MCP — verify against latest Helicone API version
Log query API is read-only — agents can analyze but not modify logs
Request logs include full prompt text — sensitive data in prompts is stored
agent_readiness.security_notes: PRIVACY: LLM prompt/response logs with potentially sensitive content. SOC2. Self-hosted option for data sovereignty. Read-only analytics API for agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: LLM observability logs with prompt/response data. Privacy-sensitive content. SOC2. Self-hosted for data sovereignty. Review retention policies.
